product_definition.name: Look Again
product_definition.category: pre-search pointing layer for the physical world
product_definition.product_category: pre-search pointing layer for the physical world
product_definition.museum_role: first proof environment, not product category
product_definition.primary_message: Before you know what to ask, XR shows where to look.
product_definition.final_message: The artwork does not change. The question does.
product_definition.core_interaction_metaphor: An expert points once. You look again.
product_definition.core_interaction_not: not an explanation panel in glasses
current_state: Native Android XR implementation is not yet complete
product_definition.core_test: Whether optical see-through Android XR can point to the right part of the physical world before the user knows what to ask.

category_reframe.guide: explains after the work is identified
category_reframe.search: answers after the question is formed

before: No guide leaves discovery to chance
Audio gives context without a precise point of attention
Phone guidance shifts attention to a copied screen image
after: The visitor remains with the physical artwork
A source-grounded lens appears beside the real artwork
A connector ties a specific artwork point to a peripheral panel
The lens can change by viewpoint without becoming a linear guide sequence

hero_transformation_model.layout: single dominant first-person museum stage with copy area and a two-act rail
hero_transformation_model.same_stage_contract: The Hero keeps the same artwork, wall, camera, and viewing distance while only the guidance layer changes.

act_1_existing_ways.autoplay_behavior: starts first and plays no guide, audio guide, and phone guide once
act_1_existing_ways.visual_treatment: subtle uncertain-focus layer; no target pulse, pointer, connector, or Look Again panel
act_1_existing_ways.purpose: show that familiar guidance modes can help but still leave attention unsettled or pulled away

sequence: Girl with a Pearl Earring x Conservator
The Arnolfini Portrait x Historian / Social Context
act_2_look_again.autoplay_behavior: appears after Existing ways completes, cycles three source-grounded examples once, then returns to a quiet thesis state
act_2_look_again.visual_treatment: uncertain-focus layer is removed; one expert cue, target, connector, and short question appear on the real artwork
act_2_look_again.purpose: show Look Again as the transformation, not a fourth equal mode

hero_transformation_model.manual_controls: top-level rail exposes Existing ways and Look Again; smaller sub-state rails expose No guide, Audio, Phone and Artist, Conservator, Historian inside their respective acts
hero_transformation_model.icons: inline SVG icons are generated for no guide, audio, phone, and Look Again controls
hero_transformation_model.first_viewport_delta_proof: The Hero states the pre-search pointing category before the visitor scrolls, and a fixed Existing vs Look Again proof block is visible before the autoplay completes.
hero_transformation_model.qa_state_contract: The main autoplay remains Existing ways -> Look Again, while query params can pin wearing, artist, restorer, social, museum, and quiet states for QA without turning expert viewpoints into a linear product flow.

display_scale_model.display_treatment: small framed painting at eye level
display_scale_model.frame_border_model: generated gilded/decorative frame texture; artwork-specific small-painting proportions
display_scale_model.source_context: Mauritshuis current collection page; Room 15; official framed-image caption

display_scale_model.display_treatment: vertical framed panel at eye level
display_scale_model.frame_border_model: generated dark Netherlandish-style frame texture; Room 52 framed panel treatment
display_scale_model.scene: generated National Gallery-informed old-master wall background
display_scale_model.source_context: National Gallery current collection page; Room 52; fifteenth-century Netherlandish frame
Museo del Cenacolo Vinciano and public catalogue dimension references

example_artwork_scope: Paintings shown in V23 are examples. The intended system can support many rights-reviewed artworks, cultural artifacts, and museum objects.

generated_backgrounds.purpose: neutral fallback gallery wall context with low floor line
generated_backgrounds.claim_limit: generated concept plate, not a real museum photo

generated_backgrounds.purpose: historic refectory end-wall context for centered wall-scale Last Supper state
generated_backgrounds.claim_limit: generated concept plate, not the actual Santa Maria delle Grazie refectory

generated_backgrounds.purpose: display-informed green wall context for Girl with a Pearl Earring
generated_backgrounds.claim_limit: generated concept plate, not Mauritshuis Room 15 reconstruction

generated_backgrounds.purpose: display-informed dark old-master wall context for The Arnolfini Portrait
generated_backgrounds.claim_limit: generated concept plate, not National Gallery Room 52 reconstruction

hero_states.label: NO GUIDE
hero_states.caption: I can see it. But I don’t know what to look for.

hero_states.label: AUDIO GUIDE
hero_states.cue: audio waveform, artwork-specific transcript bubble, and visitor confusion bubble only
hero_states.caption: Good context. But where exactly should I look?
hero_states.spatial_contract: no target pulse, no pointer, no connector, no panel

hero_states.label: PHONE GUIDE
hero_states.cue: foreground hand-held phone with copied artwork, app-like guide page, screen glow, and pixel texture
hero_states.caption: Rich information. But now I am looking at a glowing copy.
hero_states.spatial_contract: real artwork remains visible but de-emphasized behind the glowing copied screen

hero_states.label: WEARING XR
hero_states.caption: Look Again starts from the real work.

hero_states.label: ARTIST LENS
hero_states.cue: attention structure: perspective, table lines, gesture rhythm, figure placement, target pulse, and connector to a short peripheral question
hero_states.panel_question: How does the painting direct the room’s attention?
hero_states.hero_use: Hero example 1. The phone disappears and the cue attaches to the current mural.

hero_states.label: CONSERVATOR LENS
hero_states.cue: source-grounded cue spec: pearl highlight, paint edge, crop callout, material surface scan, target pulse, pointer, connector, and short peripheral question
hero_states.panel_question: How can a few marks become a pearl?
hero_states.hero_use: Hero example 2. The cue shows paint becoming illusion on the small framed painting.

hero_states.label: HISTORIAN / SOCIAL CONTEXT LENS
hero_states.cue: source-grounded cue spec: visible social-context object cluster, object-following line, target pulse, pointer, connector, and short peripheral question
hero_states.panel_question: What does wealth need us to notice?
hero_states.hero_use: Hero example 3. The cue points to objects as social evidence on the framed panel.

hero_states.label: MUSEUM HISTORY LENS
hero_states.cue: timeline in readable edge panel outside the painting

hero_states.label: QUIET VIEW
hero_states.caption: The artwork does not change. The question does.

lens_types.name: Artist lens
lens_types.definition: How the artist directs attention through composition, light, color, gaze, gesture, and pictorial staging.
lens_types.attention_shift: how the artist directs attention becomes the question; perspective is only one mechanism where appropriate

lens_types.name: Conservator lens
lens_types.definition: How the work exists as a physical object: surface, damage, repair, technique, support, pigment, and what time has changed.
lens_types.attention_shift: surface, damage, repair, support, highlight, technique, and illusion become inspectable material evidence

lens_types.name: Historian / Social Context lens
lens_types.definition: How people, objects, institutions, status, belief, trade, power, and display shape what the work meant and why it mattered.
lens_types.attention_shift: people, objects, institutions, status, belief, trade, power, and display become visible historical context

lens_types.name: Museum History
lens_types.attention_shift: collection history becomes a readable peripheral question

xr_only_mechanics.description: A curator-like pointing gesture appears in the visitor’s field of view, anchored to a real detail, then disappears.
The cue is fixed to a visible artwork detail, not to a floating web annotation.
A real artwork detail is tied to a peripheral panel.
Oblique view is used only for surface and material questions.
The cue gives a question, then returns the visitor to the artwork.

why_museums_first.summary: The problem is visible there: the visitor can stand in front of the real thing and still not know where to look.
why_museums_first.museum_role: Museum is the first proof environment, not the whole product category.
why_museums_first.failure_test: If XR distracts, darkens, or mispoints, the phone is better.

project_aura_rationale.summary: The hard question is optical, not graphical.
contrast: Google Lens can search what the visitor already noticed
Look Again tests whether optical see-through Android XR can trigger better noticing while the visitor stays with the physical work

six_month_closed_test.scope: Closed native Android XR test focused on noticing and do-not-remove validation.

validation_metrics: comparison against audio-only and phone guidance
mvp_exclusions: completed native Android XR application claim

display_reference_audit.method: official and reputable display references inform staging; runtime scenes remain display-informed approximations

artwork_specific_stage_modes.behavior: large wall-bound mural on far wall, no frame, no frame shadow, natural distant viewing
small framed wall-mounted painting at natural eye level with close viewing distance
vertical framed panel at natural eye level with enough size to inspect objects

state_specific_display_behavior.phone: foreground hand-held phone; copied artwork aspect matches current artwork; top app bar, title, metadata, chips, dense cards, scroll cue, screen glow, and pixel texture pull attention to the screen copy
state_specific_display_behavior.audio: side waveform plus artwork-specific transcript and confusion bubble; no target pulse, no pointer, no connector, no panel, and no precise spatial anchor
state_specific_display_behavior.look_again: phone disappears; cue-specific target, connector, and short artwork-specific question attach to a physically plausible detail on the real artwork

lens_matrix.cell_content_source: Each matrix cell uses the exact target_label and question from the matching cue spec.
lens_matrix.preview_rendering_source: Preview selected cue loads the matching cue spec and renders target pulse, pointer, connector, edge panel, cue type overlays, and source-grounded tag from that data.
lens_matrix.mobile_matrix_behavior: artwork cards with title, display type, thumbnail, and three stacked lens cards; no tiny four-column table
lens_matrix.cell_purpose: show where that expert viewpoint would point first and the short question it would ask
qa_must_fail_if: matrix is not visible without clicking
matrix does not have 3 artwork rows
matrix does not have 3 viewpoint columns
old Explorer remains the primary UI
